revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23991619004|time triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[en:Ikukunitama Shrine]][[fr:Ikukunitama-jinja]][[ja:生國魂神社]][[la:Ikukunitama-jinja]][[simple:Ikukunitama Shrine]][[zh:生国魂神社]] { { {{Short description|Shrine in Osaka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = {{nihongo|'''Ikukunitama Shrine'''|生國魂神社|1=Ikukunitama Shrine}} | image = File:Ikukunitama-jinja haiden-2.JPG | caption = Ikukunitama Jinja [[Honden]] | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.6651|N|135.513|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Ikutama-cho|qid=Q72930184|1=Ikutama-cho}} | part_of = {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi|qid=Q10444949|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata}} }} '''Ikukunitama Shrine''' (生國魂神社, ''Ikukunitama jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in {{ill|Tennōji-ku, Osaka|en|Tennōji-ku, Osaka|qid=Q490592|lt=Tennōji-ku, Osaka|1=Tennōji-ku, Osaka}} Prefecture, Japan. Its main festival is held annually on September 9. It was formerly an imperial shrine of the first Rank (官幣大社, ''kanpei taisha'') in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Overview== {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Ikukunitama Shrine |image = [[File:Ikukunitama-jinja haiden-2.JPG|270px]]
Haiden (Worship Hall) |location = 13-9 Ikutamachō, Tennōji-ku, Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture |coordinates = {{coord|34|39|54.41|N|135|30|45.65|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ikukunitama Shrine}} |Deities = Ikushima-no-Ōkami
Tarushima-no-Ōkami |Shintai = |Status = {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Two [[Myōjin Taisha|Myōjin Taisha]])
Former [[Kanpeitai]]
[[Beppyo Shrine]] |established = (Tradition) Around the time of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}'s accession |MainHall = Ikukunitama-zukuri |Othernames = Naniwa Taisha, Ikutama-san |Pilgrimage = 48th station on the Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi (Osaka 7th station) |festivals = {{ill|September 9|en|September 9|qid=Q2856|lt=September 9|1=September 9}} |Rituals = [[Ikukunitama Matsuri]] ([[July 11]] and [[July 12]]) |map = {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|type=point|frame-width=200}} }} {{unify characters|國}} {{coordinate list}} [[File:Ikukunitama-jinja torii.JPG|250px|thumb|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Ikukunitama Shrine''' (いくくにたまじんじゃ, in [[New Japanese characters|modern characters]]: 生国魂神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in [[Ikutamachō]], [[:en:Tennōji-ku|Tennōji-ku]], [[:en:Osaka City|Osaka City]], {{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|qid=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}. It is commonly called "Ikutama-san." It is a {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|lt=Shikinaisha}} (Myōjin Taisha). The former shrine status is [[Kanpeitai]], and it is currently a [[Beppyo Shrine]] under the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. Other names include "'''Naniwa Taisha''' (なにわのおおやしろ)" (see [[#Shrine name]]). == Overview == Located in central Osaka City, in Ikutamachō southwest of the [[Naniwa Palace]] ruins and {{ill|Osaka Castle|en|Osaka Castle|qid=Q321242|lt=Osaka Castle|1=Osaka Castle}}, the shrine was originally located on the site of today's Osaka Castle. During the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|qid=Q12554|lt=Middle Ages|1=Middle Ages}}, the [[Ōsaka Hongan-ji]] was established near the shrine's grounds, and the area prospered. However, during the construction of Osaka Castle by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, the shrine was moved to its current location in Nishitakatsumura, Nishinari District. Ikukunitama Shrine enshrines Ikushima-no-Ōkami and Tarushima-no-Ōkami, who are regarded as the spirits of the land. These deities were important in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, worshipped continuously in the imperial court, and featured in the Naniwa Eighty Islands festival, a significant ritual for new emperors. The shrine has a deep connection with these court rituals. Along with [[Ikasuri Shrine|Zama Shrine]] in [[Kudara-cho]], it is considered to have a close relationship with Naniwa Palace. The shrine has been revered through the medieval and modern eras and was Ranked as a Kanpeitai, the highest Rank under the [[Modern shrine Ranking system|modern shrine Ranking system]] before World War II, making it one of Osaka's representative ancient shrines. Though many of its old buildings and treasures have been lost to fires and war, the current main hall retains the unique architectural style of the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|qid=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}, known as "Ikukunitama-zukuri." Traditional festivals, such as the summer festival on July 11-12, continue to be held to this day. == name == {{Anchors|Shrine name}} The shrine's name has appeared in ancient documents as follows{{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}: * Ikukunitama Shrine - ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' * Naniwa Ōjinja - ''[[Shinshō Gokuchokusho]]'' * Naniwa Taisha / Nanba Taisha - ''[[Engishiki]]'' Shijisai Ainaijinsai Clause''Engishiki'' Vol. 2 (Shijisai Ge) Ainaijinsai Clause., ''Engishiki'' Rinjisai Kōushinsai Clause''Engishiki'' Vol. 3 (Rinjisai) Kōushinsai Clause. * Naniwa Ōjinja Shin - ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' * Naniwa Ikukunitama - ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' * Naniwa Ikukunitama Shrine - ''Engishiki'' Rinjisai Myōjinsai Clause''Engishiki'' Vol. 3 (Rinjisai) Myōjinsai Clause. * Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine - ''Engishiki'' [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Jinmyōchō]] In the modern era, it has also been called "Ikutamamiya," "Ikutamasha," and "Ikutama Myōjin"{{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Contemporary names include "'''Ikukunitama Shrine''' (いくくにたまじんじゃ)" and its alternate name, "'''Naniwa Taisha''' (なにわのおおやしろ)"{{r|Shrine History}}, as well as the local nickname "'''Ikutama-san''' (or "Ikutama-san")." The official website uses "Ikutama-san" and "ikutamajinja." Additionally, "Ikutama" as a place name, observed since the early modern period, derives from the shrine name{{r|Ikutama}}. == Deities == The shrine enshrines the following three deitiesShrine History.: ; Main deities :* Ikushima-no-Ōkami (生島大神, also written as 生嶋大神) :* Tarushima-no-Ōkami (足島大神, also written as 足嶋大神) ; Enshrined deities :* [[Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi-no-Ōkami]] In the ''[[Engishiki]]'' Jinmyōchō established in the 5th year of the {{ill|Engi|en|Engi|qid=Q1030330|lt=Engi|1=Engi}} era (927 AD), the enshrined deities are listed as two deities. The ''Engishiki'' lists "Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine" as meaning "the shrine of Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama deities in Naniwa," indicating two deities{{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Kami)|2000}}{{Sfn|Haruto Maeda|2010|pp=158-165}}. The current deities, Ikushima and Tarushima, are considered to be the same as these two deities, despite different names{{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}{{Sfn|Haruto Maeda|2010|pp=158-165}}. Historically, the [[Buddhist counterpart]] of the enshrined deities was considered [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi Nyorai]], and even now, Hōanji Nanbō, the former shrine temple, enshrines Yakushi Nyorai as its main deity{{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. === About the Deities === {|class="wikitable" style="background:white;font-size:85%" align="right" |+Deities enshrined at the Jingi-kan Western Court in the {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|qid=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}}Seiji Okada, "The festivals of the Jingi-kan: The Gods of the Western Court and the Miko's Service", ''New Edition: The Ancient History of Shrines'', Gakuseisha, 2011, pp.189-192. !name!!deity!!Related Shrine in Naniwa |- |style="text-align:center"|[[Hasshinden|Mikado festival Eight Deities]]||Deities overseeing the Emperor's body|| |- |style="text-align:center"|[[Ikasuri no Kami|Zama Miko festival Five Deities]]||Deities overseeing the Emperor's residence||style="text-align:center"|[[Ikasuri Shrine|Zama Shrine]] |- |style="text-align:center"|Mikado Miko festival Eight Deities||Deities overseeing the Emperor's residence gate|| |- |style="text-align:center"|'''Ikushima Miko festival Two Deities'''||Deities overseeing the Emperor's ruling land||style="text-align:center"|'''Ikukunitama Shrine''' |} Ikushima-no-Ōkami and Tarushima-no-Ōkami are deities not mentioned in myths such as the ''[[Kojiki]]'' or the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. The ''Engishiki'' Norito''Engishiki'' Vol. 8 (Norito) Kinen-sai Clause and Rokugatsu Tsukimatsuri Clause. states that Ikushima Miko worships the two deities, and the ''Engishiki'' Jinmyōchō''Engishiki'' Vol. 9 (Jinmyō Kami) Kyūchū Kami Clause. records that Ikushima Miko worships Ikushima-no-Ōkami and Tarushima-no-Ōkami at the Jingi-kan Western CourtSee ''Kyūchū-Kyōchū Shikinaisha List''.. These deities were significant, as they were worshiped by a dedicated miko in the imperial court{{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. According to the ''[[Kogo Shūi]]'', "Ikushima {{sub|is the spirit of Ōyashima (Japan), now worshiped by Ikushima Miko}}", indicating they are the spirits of Japan's land{{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}, and they are considered deities who generate and fulfill the land''Ikushima Miko festival Two Deities'', ''Shikinaisha Research Report Vol. 1'', Shikinaisha Research Association, Kōgakkan University Press, 1979.. These deities are also known for their association with the '''[[Eighty Islands festival]]''' (八十嶋祭, Yasoshima Matsuri), one of the rites for a new emperor's accession{{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. The Eighty Islands festival, held the year after a new emperor's Daijōsai, involves Ikushima Miko and others traveling from the imperial court to Naniwa, performing rituals such as shaking a box containing the emperor's clothing{{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Records show this festival was held for approximately 400 years from the Heian period's Kashō 3 (850 AD)''Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku'', Kashō 3rd year, 9th month, Jinmu, 8th day (850 AD, October 16). to the Kamakura period's Gen'nin 1 (1224 AD){{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. While the festival's purpose is debated, it is generally believed that Ikushima-no-Ōkami and Tarushima-no-Ōkami were the main deities, with the aim of incorporating the spirits of Ōyashima into the emperor's body to legitimize his rule{{Sfn|Eighty Islands festival (National History)}}. Although there is no clear evidence linking the Eighty Islands festival to Ikukunitama Shrine, it is thought that the festival's rites were related to the shrine's worship, and some suggest that the original Eighty Islands festival at Ikukunitama Shrine was adopted into the imperial court{{Sfn|New Edition Osaka City History Text Vol. 1|1988|pp=595-607}}{{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Kami)|2000}}. [[File:Ikasuri-jinja, haiden.jpg|200px|thumb|{{center|[[Ikasuri Shrine|Zama Shrine]]
(Chūō-ku, Osaka City)}}{{small|Zama Shrine's deities, like Ikukunitama Shrine's deities, were worshiped in the imperial court.}}]] In Naniwa, Zama Shrine (Chūō-ku, Osaka City, Shikinaisha) is also known as an example similar to Ikukunitama Shrine{{Sfn|New Edition Osaka City History Text Vol. 1|1988|pp=595-607}}{{r|Seiji Okada 2011}}. The deities of Zama Shrine, Zama-no-Kami, were important deities worshiped by a dedicated miko in the imperial court{{r|Kyūchū-Kyōchū Shikinaisha}}. While Ikukunitama Shrine is said to have moved from the northern end of the {{ill|Uemachi Plateau|en|Uemachi Plateau|qid=Q7877450|lt=Uemachi Plateau|1=Uemachi Plateau}} (currently the Osaka Castle area) to its current location, Zama Shrine is also thought to have been originally located near Osaka Castle{{Sfn|New Edition Osaka City History Text Vol. 1|1988|pp=595-607}}. The presence of Naniwa Palace on the northern end of the Uemachi Plateau suggests a connection between Ikukunitama Shrine, Zama Shrine, and Naniwa Palace{{Sfn|New Edition Osaka City History Text Vol. 1|1988|pp=595-607}}{{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Kami)|2000}}{{r|Seiji Okada 2011}}. Some theories propose that the origins of these worships date back to the 5th century during the [[Kawachi Dynasty]], and that the deities of the imperial court, Zama-no-Kami, and Ikushima-Tarushima-no-Kami were originally local deities (land spirits) of the Naniwa region{{Sfn|New Edition Osaka City History Text Vol. 1|1988|pp=595-607}}{{r|Seiji Okada 2011}}{{Sfn|Haruto Maeda|2010|pp=158-165}}. There are also references in ''{{ill|Kojiki-den|en|Kojiki-den|qid=Q11410835|lt=Kojiki-den|1=Kojiki-den}}'' by {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|qid=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} to the connection between the "Ikutama" shrine name and the "Ikutama" mentioned in the ''[[:en:Sendai Kuji Hongi|Sendai Kuji Hongi]]''{{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Ōmononushi-no-Ōkami, enshrined in the same hall, was enshrined later. According to the shrine tradition, it was relocated from a subordinate shrine to the main hall during the reconstruction of the shrine in the 1st year of the {{ill|Eiroku|en|Eiroku|qid=Q1143341|lt=Eiroku|1=Eiroku}} era (1558){{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. == History == === Founding === According to shrine tradition, {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, before his accession, enshrined the deities Ikushima-no-Ōkami and Tarushima-no-Ōkami, deities of the Japanese archipelago, at Naniwa Saki, near the tip of the Uemachi Plateau (currently the site of Naniwa Palace and Osaka Castle){{r|Shrine History}}{{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. The actual founding period is unclear, but it is certain that the shrine was established before the time of [[Emperor Kōtoku]], when large trees were cut down to build Naniwa Palace{{Sfn|New Edition Osaka City History Text Vol. 1|1988|pp=595-607}}{{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Kami)|2000}}. The exact location of the original shrine is unknown{{Sfn|New Edition Osaka City History Text Vol. 1|1988|pp=595-607}}. === Historical Overview === ==== Ancient Period ==== [[File:Naniwa-no-miya-ato, zenkei-2.jpg|220px|thumb|{{center|Naniwa Palace Ruins}}]] The shrine first appears in literature in ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', during the reign of [[Emperor Kōtoku]] (645-654 AD)''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Kōtoku's accession ({{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Compilation}} reference).. It mentions that Emperor Kōtoku cut down trees at Ikukunitama Shrine to build Naniwa Palace, which was seen as disrespectful to the kami{{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}''Shinpen Nihon Koten Bungaku Zenshū 4: Nihon Shoki (3)'', Shogakukan, 2003 (JapanKnowledge edition), pp.108-109.. In ''Shinshō Gokuchokusho''''Shinshō Gokuchokusho'' Vol. 10 (Shinji Sho family seal) Daidō 1 (806) registry., "Naniwa Ōjinja" is listed with two households provided as shinden from {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|qid=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province|1=Settsu Province}}{{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. The ''Six National Histories'' records that in [[:en:859 AD|859 AD]]The old calendar (Japanese calendar) for the 3rd year of the An'yū era, January 1 and January 29, correspond to February 7 and March 7 in the Julian calendar of 859 AD. "Naniwa Ikukunitama Shrine" was promoted from Junior Fifth Rank Lower to Junior Fourth Rank Lower{{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. In the same year (Jōgan 1), rain was prayed for at "Naniwa Taisha Shrine" in September''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'', Jōgan 1st year, 9th month, 8th day (859 AD, October 7) entry ({{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Compilation}} reference).{{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. In the 5th year of the Engi era (927 AD), the ''Engishiki'' Jinmyōchō''Engishiki'' Vol. 9 (Kami upper) Settsu Province East Sei District entry. lists "Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine" in East Sei District with two deities as Myōjin Taisha and states that they receive offerings during the monthly festivals, Ainaijinsai, and new harvest festivals{{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}{{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Kami)|2000}}. Additionally, in the ''Engishiki'' Rinjisai Kōushinsai Clause and Shijisai Ainaijinsai Clause, it mentions "Naniwa Taisha (Nanba Taisha) two deities" and "Naniwa Ikukunitama Shrine two deities" in the Rinjisai Myōjinsai Clause{{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}{{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Kami)|2000}}. In ''Bōan 1'' (1120 AD), the "Settsu Province Shōzei Chōan" notes the shrine received 80 bales of rice as tax revenue{{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. During the Heian period, the adjacent [[Hōanji]] gradually merged with Ikukunitama Shrine through {{ill|Syncretism|en|Syncretism|qid=Q172904|lt=Syncretism|1=Syncretism}}, becoming its [[Shingon temple]], and eventually, both became known as "Ikutamamiya Temple" and flourished. ==== Medieval Period ==== The transformations during the medieval period are unclear{{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. In ''Meiō 5'' (1496 AD), {{ill|Rennyo|en|Rennyo|qid=Q323600|lt=Rennyo|1=Rennyo}} built the Ōsaka Gobo (later the Ōsaka Hongan-ji) on the land of Hōanji, adjacent to Ikukunitama Shrine{{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. According to the ''Tenbun Diary'', in Tenbun 12 (1543), "Toi" was built on the farmland of Ikutama, and in Tenbun 13 (1544), the moat of "Ikutama Mansion" was constructed{{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. In Tenbun 15 (1546), a major relocation festival was held by Hongan-ji, with 500 swords presented to the shrine priest Yoshida{{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. The shrine served as a guardian for the Hongan-ji, but during the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|en|Siege of Osaka|qid=Q475780|lt=Siege of Osaka|1=Siege of Osaka}} in Tenshō 8 (1580), the shrine buildings were burned down{{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. ==== Early Modern Period ==== [[File:Osaka-jo, zenkei-2.jpg|220px|thumb|{{center|Osaka Castle}}]] [[File:Ikutama ema hall (Naniwa Hyakkei).jpg|220px|thumb|{{center|Ikutama Ema Hall in ''Naniwa Hyakkei''}}]] In Tenshō 11 (1583), when Toyotomi Hideyoshi began constructing Osaka Castle in Ishiyama, Ikukunitama Shrine and Hōanji were relocated to the current site{{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. While the shrine tradition states that the relocation occurred in Tenshō 13 (1585){{r|Shrine History}}, ''Gyokuyō Diary'' suggests it happened in Keichō 3 (1598){{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Hideyoshi also donated 300 koku of land to the shrine{{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. In Keichō 11, October (November 1606), {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyori|en|Toyotomi Hideyori|qid=Q313641|lt=Toyotomi Hideyori|1=Toyotomi Hideyori}} ordered the construction of shrine buildings, noted for their grandeur in ''Rokuen Diary''''Rokuen Diary'', Keichō 11, 11th month, 20th day (December 19, 1606) entry.{{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}{{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the shrine buildings constructed by Hideyori were burned down in the summer of 1615 during the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|en|Siege of Osaka|qid=Q475780|lt=Siege of Osaka|1=Siege of Osaka}}, but were later rebuilt by the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]], which also confirmed the shrine's 300 koku of land{{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. According to the ''Settsu Province High Register'' from the Kōan to Shōhō periods (1624-1648), the shrine's land was located in Shimobakachō (now Naniwa Ward){{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. Additionally, [[Tokugawa Tsunayoshi]]'s mother, [[Keishōin]], donated a certain amount of gold to the shrine{{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. ''[[Settsu Meisho Zue]]'' describes the shrine grounds and the horse-riding rituals at the time{{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. The shrine is also featured in the late Edo period book ''[[Naniwa Hyakkei]]'', which depicts its ema hall and Benten Pond. In Kōka 2 (1845), the shrine buildings were renovated{{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. ==== Modern Period ==== After the [[Meiji Restoration]], on May 14, 1871 (July 1, 1871), the shrine was Ranked as a Kanpeitai under the [[Modern shrine Ranking system]]{{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. During the Meiji [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the Shingon temple Hōanji was separated from Ikukunitama Shrine. Hōanji (or Hōanji) was a temple founded by {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|qid=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} according to temple tradition{{Sfn|Hōanji (Heibonsha)|1986}}. The ten sub-temples (Ikutama Ten Bō) were separated during the shinbutsu bunri. Hōanji Nanbō, which became independent, inherited the temple name and lamp in Shimanouchi{{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. Iōin and Hōjōin moved to Seiryuji in Ikutamateramachi, Kannonin and Shinzōin moved to Shōyūji in Kamiyamachō, Sakura Honbō moved to Enjōji in Ikoma, Nara Prefecture, Kakuenin became Sōeiji in Ikutamamae-machi, Mandarain merged with Jimei-in in Ikutamachō, Jizōin merged with Tōjiji in Ikutamachō, and Jihōin merged with Mitsu-dera in [[Shinsaibashi-suji]]{{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. In 1912 (Meiji 45), the shrine buildings were destroyed by the "Great Fire of the South," and were rebuilt in November 1913 (Taishō 2){{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. However, during the first {{ill|Bombing of Osaka|en|Bombing of Osaka|qid=Q2814395|lt=Bombing of Osaka|1=Bombing of Osaka}} on March 13-14, 1945 (Shōwa 20), the shrine buildings were once again destroyed. In 1948 (Shōwa 23), the shrine was designated as a [[Beppyo Shrine]] under the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]], and in July 1949 (Shōwa 24), the main hall was rebuilt, but it was destroyed by the Typhoon Jane in September 1950 (Shōwa 25){{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. It was later rebuilt in April 1956 (Shōwa 31) with a reinforced concrete structure{{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. The grounds included a lotus pond, which has since been filled and converted into Ikutama Park. The shrine's front approach, formerly known as "Babasaki," was also interrupted by the expansion of Tanimachi-suji{{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}}. The original site of the shrine is believed to be within Osaka Castle grounds, where there were once traces of the shrine such as a stone water basin, stone lanterns, and a sacred pine tree, but none remain today{{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}{{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Kami)|2000}}. The shrine's temporary shrine (Otabisho) is located at Honmachi-bashi, Chūō-ku, Osaka City{{r|Shrine History}} ({{Coord|34|40|56.40|N|135|30|40.50|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|name=Ikukunitama Shrine Otabisho}}). === Ranks === * January 27, 859 (An'yū 3, January 27), from Junior Fifth Rank Lower to Junior Fourth Rank Lower (''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'')''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'', An'yū 3rd year, January 27 entry (March 5, 859) ({{Harvnb|Shinto and Shrine Historical Compilation}} reference). - recorded as "Naniwa Ikukunitama Shrine." == Grounds == [[File:Ikukunitama-jinja honden.JPG|220px|thumb|{{center|main hall Roof}}]] * main hall - Rebuilt in April 1956 (Shōwa 31). Though constructed with concrete, it retains the traditional structure with a single roof of [[nagare-zukuri]] covering the five-bay-four-side main hall and seven-bay-four-side {{ill|Heiden|en|Heiden|qid=Q252034|lt=Heiden|1=Heiden}}. The roof features three gabled dormers at the front. This unique architectural style is known as "'''Ikukunitama-zukuri''' (Ikutamatsukuri, Ikutama-zukuri)" and is regarded as a remnant of the grandiose Momoyama culture.{{r|Shrine History}}{{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}{{Sfn|Ikukunitama Shrine (Heibonsha)|1986}} * Heiden - Rebuilt in 1956 (Shōwa 31). * Haiden - Rebuilt in 1956 (Shōwa 31). * Corridor - Rebuilt in 1956 (Shōwa 31). * Shinsenjo - Rebuilt in 1956 (Shōwa 31). * Ritual Hall - Rebuilt in 1956 (Shōwa 31). * Shrine Office Ikukunitama-jinja Shamusho.jpg|Shrine Office Ikukunitama-jinja angu.jpg|Temporary Shrine (Honmachi-bashi, Chūō-ku, Osaka City) Ikukunitama-jinja otabisho-ato.jpg|Former Otabisho (Osaka Castle, Chūō-ku, Osaka City)
{{small|Located near the Ōtemon gate of Osaka Castle, reconstructed in 1932 (Shōwa 7).}} == Auxiliary Shrines == * Kōtaijin Shrine ** deity: [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] ** festival: October 17 * Sumiyoshi Shrine ** deity: [[Sumiyoshi sanjin|Sokotutsuo-no-Mikoto, Nakatsutsuo-no-Mikoto, Uwatsutsuo-no-Mikoto]] ** festival: July 31 * Tenmangū ** deity: {{ill|Sugawara no Michizane|en|Sugawara no Michizane|qid=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} ** festival: March 25 * Jōhō Hachimangū (Kitamuki Hachimangū) ** deity: {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|qid=Q317997|lt=Honda Wake-no-Mikoto|1=Emperor Ōjin}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Okinaga Tarashihime-no-Mikoto|1=Empress Jingū}}, [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori Hime-no-Mikoto]] ** festival: September 15 *: The name "Kitamuki" means "north-facing" and indicates the shrine was established as a guardian for the north-facing Osaka Castle. It was originally located near the lotus pond in front of the torii gate{{r|Shrine History}}. * Fuhigo Shrine ** deity: [[Ame-no-Mahitotsu-no-Kami]], [[Ishikoridome|Ishikoridome-no-Mikoto]], [[Kagu-tsuchi|Kagutsuchi-no-Kami]] ** festival: November 8 * Yadukuri Miōya Shrine ** deity: [[Te-Oki-Hohohi-no-Kami]], [[Hiko-Sashikuni-no-Kami]] ** festival: April 11 * Jōruri Shrine ** deity: {{ill|Chikamatsu Monzaemon|en|Chikamatsu Monzaemon|qid=Q324986|lt=Chikamatsu Monzaemon|1=Chikamatsu Monzaemon}} and {{ill|Bunraku|en|Bunraku|qid=Q243170|lt=Bunraku|1=Bunraku}} (Jōruri) associates ** festival: Vernal Equinox Day, Autumnal Equinox Day * Shigino Shrine ** deity: [[Ichikishima-hime|Ichikishima-hime-no-Kami]], [[Ōmiya-no-Me|Ōmiya-no-Me-no-Kami]], Yodohime-no-Kami ** festival: Mid-April and October on the day of the serpent *: Once enshrined on Benten Island (current site of {{ill|Osaka Business Park|en|Osaka Business Park|qid=Q2418640|lt=Osaka Business Park|1=Osaka Business Park}}), it was revered by {{ill|Yodo-dono|en|Yodo-dono|qid=Q247408|lt=Yodo-dono|1=Yodo-dono}}. When Benten Island was acquired, the shrine was moved to Ikukunitama Shrine grounds{{r|Shrine History}}. * Genkurō Inari Shrine ** deity: Genkurō Inari Daigongen, Hachibei Daigongen ** festival: First Day of the Horse in March * Inari Shrine ** deity: [[Ukanomitama|Ukano Mitama-no-Kami]] ** festival: First Day of the Horse in March, September 21 *: Split from [[Yūtoku Inari Shrine]] (Kashima City, Saga Prefecture). * Seichin Shrine ** deity: [[Kotoshironushi-no-Kami]], [[Hime-no-Kami]] ** festival: April 7, October 1 *: Originally a "Benzaiten Shrine" enshrined at the lotus pond on the main approach, renamed Seichin Shrine at the beginning of the Meiji era and moved to its current location after the postwar filling of the pond. Ikukunitama-jinja Kotai-jinja.JPG|Kōtaijin Shrine Ikukunitama-jinja Sumiyoshi-jinja and Tenman-gu.JPG|Sumiyoshi Shrine (left) and Tenmangū (right) Ikukunitama-jinja keidaisha-1.JPG|(from left) Jōhō Hachimangū, Fuhigo Shrine, Yadukuri Miōya Shrine, Jōruri Shrine Ikukunitama-jinja keidaisha-2.JPG|(from right) Shigino Shrine, Genkurō Inari Shrine, Inari Shrine Ikukunitama-jinja Seichin-sha.JPG|Seichin Shrine == festivals == === festivals ===
List of festivals
Ikukunitama Shrine hosts the following festivals{{r|Shrine History}}. * Monthly ** Tsukinami-sai (1st, 9th, and 15th of every month) * January ** New Year festival (January 1-3) ** Wakana Uzue-sai (January 7) ** Omikuji Shinji (January 13) - Shrine ritual of Jōhō Hachimangū. ** Sagichō Shinji (Tondo-yaki) (January 15) * February ** Setsubun-sai, Kiyomoto Yaki-nō Shinji (February 3) ** Kigensai (February 11) ** Kinen-sai (February 17) * March ** Jōruri Shrine Spring festival (Vernal Equinox Day in March) ** Inari-sai (First Day of the Horse in March) * April ** Mikagura-sai (April 7) ** Restoration Memorial festival (April 8) ** Shigino Shrine Spring festival (mid-April on the day of the serpent) ** Yadukuri Miōya Shrine festival (April 11) * May ** Hashi Uma Shinji (May 5) - Shrine ritual of Jōhō Hachimangū. * June ** Ōharae (June 30) * July ** Tanabata Yaki-nō-sai (July 8) ** [[Ikukunitama Matsuri]] (July 11-12) * August ** Osaka Takigi Nō (August 11-12) * September ** '''Reitaisai''' (September 9) ** Hikohachi Matsuri (First Saturday and Sunday of September) ** Jōruri Shrine Autumn festival (Autumnal Equinox Day in September) * October ** Autumn festival (October 15) ** Kōtaijin Shrine festival (October 17) ** Shigino Shrine Autumn festival (mid-October on the day of the serpent) ** Kensha-sai (October 28) * November ** Meiji-sai (November 3) ** Fuhigo Shrine festival (November 8) ** Shichi-Go-San festival (November 15) ** Niinamesai (November 23) * December ** Yuino-shiho-yaki-nō Shinji (December 13) ** Susuharai-sai (December 22) ** Tenchō-sai (December 23) ** Ōharae (December 31)
=== Major festivals === ; Wakana Uzue-sai : January 7. A festival combining Wakana-sai and [[Uzue-sai]]. During the ritual to ward off disasters, offerings of young greens are made, and large radishes adorned with young pine branches are placed on both sides of the offering table. Plum branches are used instead of uzue branches{{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. ; Hashi Uma Shinji : May 5. A shrine ritual of Jōhō Hachimangū. Originally, [[yabusame]] (horseback archery) was held at the main approach called "Babasaki" in front of the torii, depicted in ''Settsu Meisho Zue'' and ''Naniwa Kankō'', but now, the ritual and various events are held{{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. ; [[Ikukunitama Matsuri]] : July 11 (Eve of the festival) and 12 (Main festival). One of Osaka's three major summer festivalsThe "Three Major Summer festivals of Osaka" typically include the [[Tenjin Matsuri]] of [[Osaka Tenmangū]], the [[Sumiyoshi Matsuri]] of [[Sumiyoshi-taisha|Sumiyoshi Taisha]], and either Ikutama Summer festival or the [[Aizen Matsuri]] of the [[Aizen-do|Shitennoji Betsuin Aizen-do]].. Before World War II, the mikoshi procession extended to Osaka Castle, and the parade, known as "land procession," was a grand spectacle comparable to the "boat procession" of the Tenjin Matsuri{{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}[https://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/tennoji/cmsfiles/contents/0000404/404034/tennoji17-07_p02.pdf Tennōji Ward Public Relations, Heisei 29 (2017) July 1, No. 254] Tennōji Ward (accessed July 15, 2021). The procession was halted due to the loss of ritual implements and historical documents during the {{ill|Bombing of Osaka|en|Bombing of Osaka|qid=Q2814395|lt=Bombing of Osaka|1=Bombing of Osaka}} in World War II, but in 1990 (Heisei 2), a simplified mikoshi was created, and the procession was resumed by vehicle. In 2014 (Heisei 26), a new palanquin was made with public donations, fully restoring the procession. Due to the COVID-19 pandemic, the procession and other events were canceled from 2020 to 2022, with only the rituals being performed[https://www.nnn.co.jp/dainichi/news/210713/20210713020.html "Ikukunitama festival Main Day, Gengen Shinden festival at Osaka Castle"] Osaka Daily News, July 13, 2021 (accessed July 15, 2021)[https://www.ikutamajinja.jp/pdf/book_20200629.pdf "Cancellation of Ikukunitama festival Procession"] Ikukunitama Shrine, April 24, 2021 (accessed July 15, 2021){{Cite tweet|author=Ikukunitama Shrine (official) |user=ikutamajinja |number=1546345760249171968 |title=The Ikukunitama festival on July 11-12 will be primarily conducted with rituals this year. |date=2022-07-11 |accessdate=2022-07-25}}. ; Osaka Takigi Nō : August 11-12. Based on a tradition of nō theater being performed at the shrine before the construction of Osaka Castle, a torch-lit nō performance is held{{r|Shrine History}}. ; Autumn festival : October 15. Known as "Hatsuho Matsuri" before World War II, it involved a ritual where 12 women from the shrine parish offered the first rice harvest to the deities. Today, it is a ceremony where offerings from parishioners are presented with the shrine food{{Sfn|Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine Two Shrines (Shikinaisha)|1977}}. == In Literature == * {{ill|Ihara Saikaku|en|Ihara Saikaku|qid=Q352561|lt=Ihara Saikaku|1=Ihara Saikaku}}'s "Ikutama Manku" - A collection of ten thousand haiku poems held at the shrine's Nanbō. * {{ill|Chikamatsu Monzaemon|en|Chikamatsu Monzaemon|qid=Q324986|lt=Chikamatsu Monzaemon|1=Chikamatsu Monzaemon}}'s "Ikutama Shinju" - Set in the grounds of Ikukunitama Shrine. * Chikamatsu Monzaemon's "{{ill|The Love Suicides at Sonezaki|en|The Love Suicides at Sonezaki|qid=Q892262|lt=The Love Suicides at Sonezaki|1=The Love Suicides at Sonezaki}}" - The opening scene is set at Ikukunitama Shrine. * {{ill|Haruko Wakita|en|Haruko Wakita|qid=Q5675756|lt=Haruko Wakita|1=Haruko Wakita}} (lyrics), [[Bunzo Otsuki]] (music) "Ikukunitama (Ikutama)" - A nō performance that recreates the Eighty Islands festival at Ikukunitama Shrine. * [[:en:Arisu Arisugawa|Arisu Arisugawa]]'s "Shingonsaka" - A short story in the collection ''[[Maboroshizaka]]'', set in the Tennōji Seven Slopes, with Ikukunitama Shrine as a key location. == Pilgrimage Sites == ; [[Shinbutsu Reijō Junpai no Michi]] : 47 [[Hōrakuji (Osaka City)|Hōrakuji]] * '''48 Ikukunitama Shrine''' - 49 [[Ikasuri Shrine|Zama Shrine]] == Information == ; location * 13-9 Ikutamachō, Tennōji-ku, Osaka City, Osaka Prefecture ; Transportation * Railways ** {{ill|Osaka Municipal Subway|en|Osaka Municipal Subway|qid=Q303026|lt=Osaka Metro|1=Osaka Metro}} [[:en:Osaka Metro Tanimachi Line|Tanimachi Line]] and [[:en:Osaka Metro Sennichimae Line|Sennichimae Line]] {{ill|Tanimachi Kyūchōme Station|en|Tanimachi Kyūchōme Station|qid=Q1041977|lt=Tanimachi Kyūchōme Station|1=Tanimachi Kyūchōme Station}} (approx. 4 minutes on foot) ** [[:en:Osaka Metro Sakaisuji Line|Sakaisuji Line]] and Sennichimae Line {{ill|Nippombashi Station|en|Nippombashi Station|qid=Q1040630|lt=Nippombashi Station|1=Nippombashi Station}} and [[:en:Kintetsu Corporation|Kintetsu]] [[:en:Kintetsu-Nippombashi Station|Kintetsu-Nippombashi Station]] (approx. 7 minutes on foot) ** Kintetsu {{ill|Ōsaka Uehommachi Station|en|Ōsaka Uehommachi Station|qid=Q872600|lt=Ōsaka Uehommachi Station|1=Ōsaka Uehommachi Station}} (approx. 10 minutes on foot) ; Surrounding Area * [[Kōzu Shrine]] * [[Hōanji]] ({{Coord|34|40|10.15|N|135|30|27.48|E|region:JP-27_type:landmark|name=Hōanji Nanbō (former shrine temple)}}) - Originally the shrine temple of Ikukunitama Shrine. ==Notes== == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Further Reading == * [[Nobumaro Noda]], ''Kanpei Taisha Ikukunitama Shrine History'', 1919. ** [{{NDLDC|943707}} ''Kanpei Taisha Ikukunitama Shrine History''] (National Diet Library Digital Collections). * ''[[Kojiruien]]'', compiled by the Jingūji Office, Ikukunitama Shrine entry. ** [{{NDLDC|1873551/163}} ''Kojiruien Vol. 9''] (National Diet Library Digital Collections), see 163-164 frames. * Motohiko Atsutsu and Yoshihiko Umeda (editors and supervisors), ''Shinto Dictionary'', Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, p. 6 * [[Eiichiro Shirai]] and [[Masakuni Doki]] (editors), ''Shrine Dictionary'', Tokyo-dō Shuppan, 1979, p. 23 * Haruhira Ueyama and others, ''Japan Shrine Overview'', Shinjinbutsu Ōraisha, 1992, pp. 192-193 * ''Shinto no Hon'', Gakken, 1992, p. 222 == External Links == {{Commonscat|Ikukunitama-jinja}} {{osm box|n|439280593}} * [https://www.ikutamajinja.jp/ Ikukunitama Shrine] - Ikukunitama Shrine (official site) * [https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/21coe/?J_ID=50802 Naniwa Iki-kuni Saku-kuni-tama Shrine] - Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program "Shinto and Shrine Historical Compilation" ==External links== {{commons category|Ikukunitama-jinja}} *[http://ikutamajinja.jp Official website] *[http://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2014/06/24/national/osakas-ikukunitama-shrine-set-revive-ritual-first-time-70-years/ Osaka's Ikukunitama Shrine set to revive ritual for first time in 70 years] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|生國魂神社|version=101361650|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[en:Ikukunitama Shrine]] [[ja:生國魂神社]] {{Commons category|Ikukunitama-jinja}} [[fr:Ikukunitama-jinja]] [[la:Ikukunitama-jinja]] [[simple:Ikukunitama Shrine]] [[zh:生国魂神社]] {{Commons category|Ikukunitama-jinja}} [[fr:Ikukunitama-jinja]] [[la:Ikukunitama-jinja]] [[simple:Ikukunitama Shrine]] [[zh:生国魂神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11574477}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Tennoji-ku, Osaka]] [[Category:History of Tennoji-ku, Osaka]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Pages linked to by wikidata]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Settsu Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Osaka]] [[Category:Tennōji-ku, Osaka]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Shrine receiving Tsukinami-sai and Niiname-sai and Ainame-sai offerings]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Pages with 50+ untranslated japanese characters]]
